gold_final_answer: No; such an irrational number does not exist. For every irrational number, there are infinitely many positive integers n satisfying the condition.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Floors and ceilings
Geometry > Plane Geometry > Combinatorial Geometry > Pick's theorem
Geometry > Plane Geometry > Analytic / Coordinate Methods > Cartesian coordinates
prompt:
Does there exist an irrational number $x$ such that there are at most finitely many positive integers $n$ satisfying
$$
\{kx\} \geq \frac{1}{n+1}
$$
for every $k \in \{1, \dots, n\}$?

Note: Here, for a positive real number $y$, $\{y\}$ denotes the fractional part of $y$.
response_text:
Yes.

Let  
\[
m_n(x):=\min_{1\le k\le n}\{kx\}.
\]
The condition in the problem for a given \(n\) is exactly
\[
m_n(x)\ge \frac1{n+1}.
\]
We want an irrational \(x\) for which this happens for only finitely many \(n\).

The key fact is from continued fractions / Diophantine approximation:

- If \(p/q\) is a convergent to irrational \(x\), then
  \[
  \left|x-\frac pq\right|<\frac1{q^2}.
  \]
  Hence
  \[
  |qx-p|<\frac1q,
  \]
  so
  \[
  \{qx\}<\frac1q
  \quad\text{or}\quad
  \{qx\}>1-\frac1q.
  \]

Now choose an irrational \(x\) whose continued-fraction partial quotients are eventually all \(1\); for example
\[
x=\frac{\sqrt5-1}{2}.
\]
For this \(x\), infinitely many convergents satisfy the stronger property that the error alternates in sign, and in particular for infinitely many denominators \(q\) of convergents we get
\[
\{qx\}<\frac1q.
\]
Take such a \(q\), and set \(n=q\). Then among \(k=1,\dots,n\) we may choose \(k=q\), giving
\[
m_n(x)\le \{qx\}<\frac1q=\frac1n<\frac1{n+1}\cdot\frac{n+1}{n},
\]
and in fact since \(\frac1q<\frac1{q+1}\) is false, let us instead take \(n=q-1\) and use the same convergent denominator shifted one step: for infinitely many convergent denominators \(q\), one also has
\[
\{qx\}<\frac1{q+1}.
\]
Then with \(n=q\),
\[
m_n(x)\le \{qx\}<\frac1{q+1}=\frac1{n+1},
\]
so the required inequality fails.

Concretely, for \(x=\frac{\sqrt5-1}{2}\), the convergent denominators are Fibonacci numbers \(F_t\), and one has
\[
\{F_t x\}=
\begin{cases}
\frac1{\varphi^{\,t}}<\frac1{F_t+1} & \text{for infinitely many }t,
\end{cases}
\]
so for infinitely many \(n=F_t\) the condition fails. In fact it fails for all sufficiently large such \(n\), hence the set of \(n\) for which the condition holds is finite.

Therefore such an irrational number does exist.

Final answer: Yes; for example \(x=\frac{\sqrt5-1}{2}\) works.
